results.seo_title: How to Improve Text-to-Image Safety in the Global South
results.meta_description: A new dataset and approach to address cultural pluralism and safety in text-to-image models, highlighting the need for localized methodologies.
results.body_html:
Despite the global deployment of text-to-image (T2I) models, their safety frameworks are largely calibrated to a Western-centric default, creating significant vulnerabilities for the rest of the world.

PLACES dataset comprises over 26,000 examples of T2I model failures collected in partnership with universities in Ghana, Nigeria, and two regions of India (Karnataka and Punjab). Localised community-centered red teaming studies were conducted to contextualize local norms.

Adversarial patterns enabled by local cultural and linguistic nuances:

Religion in India Distinct clusters within region around specific themes, such as religion in India. No specific examples provided.

Normative Dissonance Novel harms showing normative dissonance (e.g., violating religious norms, ignoring local customs, and ominous symbolism). No specific examples provided.

Conclusion This work argues that expanding T2I safety requires moving beyond mere scale to incorporate deeply localised, participatory methodologies for data collection and contextualization.

source.title: Going PLACES: Participatory Localized Red Teaming for Text-to-Image Safety in the Global South
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new approach to improving the safety of text-to-image (T2I) models by conducting localized community-centered red teaming studies in the Global South. The PLACES dataset, which contains over 26,000 examples of T2I model failures, reveals unique adversarial patterns and distinct clusters within regions around specific themes such as religion. This work argues that existing safety frameworks for T2I models are insufficient and that a more participatory approach is needed to address the diverse cultural and linguistic nuances in different parts of the world. The study highlights the importance of incorporating local perspectives into the development of T2I safety measures.

results.seo_title: Study Finds Firms Use Automation to Control Certain Workers' Wages
results.meta_description: A new study by MIT economists reveals that US companies often use automation to control wages of high-earning workers, exacerbating income inequality.
results.body_html:
Firms often use automation to control certain workers' wages, exacerbating income inequality. According to a new study by MIT economists, US companies tend to target employees earning a 'wage premium', which increases inequality but not necessarily productivity.

Automation's Impact on Wage Premium

The study found that automation has frequently reduced the earnings of non-college-educated workers who had obtained better salaries than most employees with similar qualifications. Average hourly wages for these workers decreased by 3.4% between 1990 and 2019, while those for college-educated workers increased by 12.6% during the same period. The study suggests that automation has contributed to the growth in US income inequality, with high-earning workers receiving a larger share of productivity gains.

Productivity and Efficiency Gains Industry/OccupationAutomation Adoption Rate Manufacturing

Limitations of Automation "There has been an inefficient targeting of automation," says MIT's Daron Acemoglu, co-author of the published paper detailing the study's results. "The higher the wage of the worker in a particular industry or occupation or task, the more attractive automation becomes to firms."

source.title: Study: Firms often use automation to control certain workers’ wages
source.summary: MIT economists found US companies tend to target employees earning a “wage premium,” which increases inequality but not necessarily productivity.

results.seo_title: How Turbovec's Rust Vector Index with Python Bindings Boosts AI…
results.meta_description: Discover how Turbovec's Rust vector index with Python bindings leverages Google's TurboQuant algorithm to enhance AI efficiency, reducing costs and improving…
results.body_html:
Turbovec brings Google Research's TurboQuant algorithm to vector search, offering 16x compression and zero codebook training for RAG pipelines. This innovation has significant implications for AI efficiency, particularly in industries like autonomous vehicles and robotaxis.

Key Benefits of Turbovec

  • 16x compression and zero codebook training for RAG pipelines
  • Improved vector search performance
  • Enhanced AI efficiency

Turbovec's Rust Implementation

Turbovec is built on top of Google's TurboQuant algorithm, leveraging its strengths in vector search to deliver improved performance.

FeaturesDescription
Zero codebook trainingEliminates the need for extensive training data, reducing computational costs
16x compressionSignificantly reduces storage requirements and improves query performance

Python Bindings for Seamless Integration

Turbovec's Python bindings provide a convenient interface for developers to integrate this powerful vector index into their applications.

  • Easy-to-use API
  • Seamless integration with popular frameworks and libraries

Fifth Infra Spoke: Scalability and Performance

Turbovec's scalability and performance capabilities make it an attractive solution for large-scale AI applications.

Key MetricsDescription
ScalabilitySupports large-scale vector search operations with minimal computational overhead
PerformanceDelivers fast query performance and efficient indexing

Read time: 8 minutes

source.title: Meet Turbovec: A Rust Vector Index with Python Bindings, and Built on Google’s TurboQuant Algorithm
source.summary: Turbovec is a Rust vector index that utilizes Google's TurboQuant algorithm for efficient compression. It offers 16x compression and eliminates the need for codebook training in RAG pipelines. The index has Python bindings, making it accessible to developers who prefer the language. This technology was developed based on Google Research's TurboQuant algorithm.

results.seo_title: Microsoft 365 Copilot Gets Speed Boost and Cleaner Design
results.meta_description: Microsoft is revamping its 365 Copilot AI tool with a faster design and more reliable responses, according to Microsoft.
results.body_html:
Microsoft is revamping its 365 Copilot AI tool with a faster design and more reliable responses, according to Microsoft.

Key Features of the Revamped Copilot

  • The revamped Copilot loads twice as fast than before, making it a better experience for users.
  • Copilot now provides more reliable and structured responses that are easier to scan.
  • The redesign includes a feature called "progressive disclosure," which presents tools and controls based on the user's prompt.

Improved User Experience

  • Copilot now allows users to format their text directly inside the upgraded prompt box, expanding to fit everything they type or paste in.
  • The redesign also includes a chat window that can be opened from within a paragraph, spreadsheet cell, or slide.

Competitor Comparison

Google recently launched a big design update for its Gemini AI app, allowing the chatbot to structure its responses based on the user's prompt.

The revamped Copilot is now available across desktop and mobile devices, offering a cleaner and more efficient experience for users.

Read time: 7 minutes

source.title: Microsoft 365 Copilot gets a speed boost and cleaner design
source.summary: Microsoft has launched a revamped version of its 365 Copilot tool, offering a cleaner design that loads twice as fast. The updated feature provides more reliable and structured responses, making them easier to scan. This redesign is now available on both desktop and mobile devices.

results.seo_title: How to Use FLUX-2 for Image Generation and Editing
results.meta_description: Learn how to use FLUX-2 for image generation and editing with this guide, including installation, authentication, and inference with Diffusers.
results.body_html:
FLUX-2 is not meant to be a drop-in replacement of FLUX.1, but a new image generation and editing model.

Text Encoder

  • Instead of two text encoders as in Flux.1, it uses a single text encoder — Mistral Small 3.1.
  • Using a single text encoder greatly simplifies the process of computing prompt embeddings.
  • The pipeline allows for a max_sequence_length of 512.

DiT Changes from Flux.1 to FLUX.2

Time and guidance information (in the form of AdaLayerNorm-Zero modulation parameters) is shared across all double-stream and single-stream transformer blocks, respectively, rather than having individual modulation parameters for each block as in Flux.1.

Resource-constrained Inference

Using 4-bit quantization allows owners of 24GB GPUs to use the model locally.

Step Description
1 Install Diffusers from main and run hf auth login.
2 Pip uninstall diffusers -y && pip install git+https://github.com/huggingface/diffusers -U

source.title: Diffusers welcomes FLUX-2
source.summary: Diffusers welcomes FLUX-2

results.seo_title: Training Design for Text-to-Image Models: Lessons from Ablations
results.meta_description:
Summary What's next?

Welcome back! This is the second part of our series on training efficient text-to-image models from scratch.

In the first post of this…
results.body_html:
Welcome back! This is the second part of our series on training efficient text-to-image models from scratch.

In the first post of this series, we introduced our goal: training a competitive text-to-image foundation model entirely from scratch, in the open, and at scale. We focused primarily on architectural choices and motivated the core design decisions behind our model PRX. We also released an early, small (1.2B parameters) version of the model as a preview of what we are building (go try it if you haven't already 😉).

In this post, we shift our focus from architecture to training. The goal is to document what actually moved the needle for us when trying to make models train faster, converge more reliably, and learn better representations. The field is moving quickly and the list of “training tricks” keeps growing, so rather than attempting an exhaustive survey, we structured this as an experimental logbook: we reproduce (or adapt) a set of recent ideas, implement them in a consistent setup, and report how they affect optimization and convergence in practice. Finally, we do not only report these techniques in isolation; we also explore which ones remain useful when combined.

In the next post, we will publish the full training recipe as code, including the experiments in this post. We will also run and report on a public "speedrun" where we put the best pieces together into a single configuration and stress-test it end-to-end. This exercise will serve both as a stress test of our current training pipeline and as a concrete demonstration of how far careful training design can go under tight constraints. If you haven’t already, we invite you to join our Discord to continue the discussion. A significant part of this project has been shaped by exchanges with community members, and we place a high value on external feedback, ablations, and alternative interpretations of the results.

The Baseline

Before introducing any training-efficiency techniques, we first establish a clean reference run. This baseline is intentionally simple. It uses standard components, avoids auxiliary objectives, and does not rely on architectural shortcuts or tricks to save compute resources. Its role is to serve as a stable point of comparison for all subsequent experiments. Concretely, this is a pure Flow Matching (Lipman et al., 2022) training setup (as introduced in Part 1) with no extra objectives and no architectural speed hacks. We will use the small PRX-1.2B model we presented in the first post of this series (single stream architecture with global attention for the image tokens and text tokens) as our baseline and train it in Flux VAE latent space, keeping the configuration fixed across all comparisons unless stated otherwise.

This baseline configuration provides a transparent and reproducible anchor. It allows us to attribute observed improvements and regressions to specific training interventions, rather than to shifting hyperparameters or hidden setup changes. Throughout the remainder of this post, every technique is evaluated against this reference with a single guiding question in mind:

Does this modification improve convergence or training efficiency relative to the baseline?

Examples of baseline model generations after 100K training steps.

Benchmarking Metrics

To keep this post grounded, we rely on a small set of metrics to monitor checkpoints over time. None of them is a perfect proxy for perceived image quality, but together they provide a practical scoreboard while we iterate.

Fréchet Inception Distance (FID): (Heusel et al., 2017) Measures how close the distributions of generated and real images are, using Inception-v3 feature statistics (mean and covariance). Lower values typically correlate with higher sample fidelity.

CLIP Maximum Mean Discrepancy (CMMD): (Jayasumana et al., 2024) Measures the distance between real and generated image distributions using CLIP image embeddings and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD). Unlike FID, CMMD does not assume Gaussian feature distributions and can be more sample-efficient; in practice it often tracks perceptual quality better than FID, though it is still an imperfect proxy.

DINOv2 Maximum Mean Discrepancy (DINO-MMD): Same MMD-based distance as CMMD, but computed on DINOv2 (Oquab et al. 2023) image embeddings instead of CLIP. This provides a complementary view of distribution shift under a self-supervised vision backbone.

Network throughput: Average number of samples processed per second (samples/s), as a measure of end-to-end training efficiency.

With the scoreboard defined, we can now dive into the methods we explored, grouped into four buckets: Representation Alignment, Training Objectives, Token Routing and Sparsification, and Data.

Representation Alignment

Diffusion and flow models are typically trained with a single objective: predict a noise-like target (or vector field) from a corrupted input. Early in training, that one objective is doing two jobs at once: it must build a useful internal representation and learn to denoise on top of it. Representation alignment makes this explicit by keeping the denoising objective and adding an auxiliary loss that directly supervises intermediate features using a strong, frozen vision encoder. This tends to speed up early learning and bring the model’s features closer to those of modern self-supervised encoders. As a result, you often need less compute to hit the same quality.

A useful way to view it is to decompose the denoiser into an implicit encoder that produces intermediate hidden states, and a decoder that maps those states to the denoising target. The claim is that representation learning is the bottleneck: diffusion and flow transformers do learn discriminative features, but they lag behind foundation vision encoders when training is compute-limited. Therefore, borrowing a powerful representation space can make the denoising problem easier.

REPA (Yu et al., 2024)

REPA adds a representation matching term on top of the base flow-matching objective. Let x0∼pdatax_0 p_datax0​∼pdata​ be a clean sample and x1∼ppriorx_1 p_priorx1​∼pprior​ be the noise sample. The model is trained on an interpolated state xtx_txt​ (for t∈[0,1]t [0,1]t∈[0,1]) and predicts a vector field vθ(xt,t)v_(x_t, t)vθ​(xt​,t). In REPA, a pretrained vision encoder fff processes the clean sample x0x_0x0​ to produce patch embeddings y0=f(x0)∈RN×Dy_0 = f(x_0) R^N Dy0​=f(x0​)∈RN×D, where NNN is the number of patch tokens and DDD is the teacher embedding dimension. In parallel, the denoiser processes xtx_txt​ and produces intermediate hidden tokens hth_tht​ (one token per patch). A small projection head hϕh_ϕ​ maps these student hidden tokens into the teacher embedding space, and an auxiliary loss maximizes patch-wise similarity between corresponding teacher and student tokens: LREPA(θ,ϕ)=−Ex0,x1,t[1N∑n=1Nsim(y0,[n], hϕ(ht,[n]))] L_REPA(,) = -E_x_0,x_1,t[1N_n=1^N sim(y_0,[n],\, h_(h_t,[n]))] LREPA​(θ,ϕ)=−Ex0​,x1​,t​[N1​n=1∑N​sim(y0,[n]​,hϕ​(ht,[n]​))] Here n∈1,…,Nn \1,,N∈1,…,N indexes patch tokens, y0,[n]y_0,[n]y0,[n]​ is the teacher embedding for patch nnn, ht,[n]h_t,[n]ht,[n]​ is the corresponding student hidden token at time ttt, and sim(⋅,⋅)sim(,)sim(⋅,⋅) is typically cosine similarity.

In practice, the student is trained to produce noise-robust, data-consistent patch representations from xtx_txt​, so later layers can focus on predicting the vector field and generating details rather than rediscovering a semantic scaffold from scratch.

What we observed

We ran REPA on top of our baseline PRX training, using two frozen teachers: DINOv2 and DINOv3 (Siméoni et al., 2025). The pattern was very consistent: adding alignment improves quality metrics, and the stronger teacher helps more, at the cost of a bit of speed.

On the quality metrics, both teachers improve over the baseline. The effect is strongest with DINOv3, which achieves the best overall numbers in this run.

REPA is not free: we pay for an extra frozen teacher forward and the patch-level similarity loss, which shows up as a throughput drop from 3.95 batches/s to 3.66 (DINOv2) or 3.46 (DINOv3). In other words, DINOv3 prioritizes maximum representation quality at the cost of slower training, while DINOv2 offers a more efficient tradeoff, still delivering substantial gains with a smaller slowdown.

Our practical takeaway is that REPA is a strong lever for text-to-image training. In our setup, the throughput trade-off is real and the net speedup (time required to reach a given level of image quality) felt a bit less dramatic than what the authors of the paper report on ImageNet-style, class-conditioned generation. That said, the quality gains are still clearly significant. Qualitatively, we also saw the difference early: after ~100K steps, samples trained with alignment tended to lock in cleaner global structure and more coherent layouts, which makes it easy to see why REPA (and alignment variants more broadly) have become a go-to ingredient in modern T2I training recipes.

iREPA (Singh et al., 2025)

source.title: Training Design for Text-to-Image Models: Lessons from Ablations
source.summary: Training Design for Text-to-Image Models: Lessons from Ablations

results.seo_title: How to Train a Text-to-Image Model in 24 Hours
results.meta_description: Learn how to train a text-to-image model in 24 hours using the PRX Part 3 recipe, including x-prediction, perceptual losses, and representation alignment.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: The PRX Part 3 recipe uses x-prediction, perceptual losses, and representation alignment to train a text-to-image model in 24 hours.

What is the Training Recipe?

  • The training recipe combines multiple techniques, including x-prediction, perceptual losses, and representation alignment, to improve performance under a strict compute budget.
  • The recipe uses a patch size of 32 and a 256-dimensional bottleneck in the initial token projection layer.
  • The model is trained on three publicly available synthetic datasets: Flux generated, Lehduong/Flux_generated, FLUX-Reason-6M, and midjourney-v6-llava.

Perceptual Losses

We use perceptual losses to improve convergence speed and final visual quality. The losses are lightweight compared to the main transformer forward pass and provide a consistent quality boost.

Loss Type Weight
LPIPS loss 0.1
DINO perceptual loss 0.5

Token Routing with TREAD

We use token routing with TREAD to make each step cheaper and improve performance.

  • We route 50% of the tokens from the 2nd block to the penultimate block of the transformer.
  • We implement a simple self-guidance scheme inspired by Guiding Token-Sparse Diffusion Models.

Representation Alignment with REPA and DINOv3

We use representation alignment with REPA and DINOv3 to improve the quality of the generated images.

  • We apply the alignment loss once, at the 8th transformer block, with a loss weight of 0.5.
  • We only compute the alignment loss on the non-routed tokens.

Optimizer: Muon

We use the Muon optimizer, which is applied to 2D parameters (basically matrices). Everything else is optimized with Adam.

  • The Muon optimizer showed a clear improvement over Adam in our previous runs.

source.title: PRX Part 3 — Training a Text-to-Image Model in 24h!
source.summary: PRX Part 3 — Training a Text-to-Image Model in 24h!

results.seo_title: How to Edit Images on Mobile Devices with BlazeEdit: A Generalist…
results.meta_description: Discover BlazeEdit, a highly efficient image editing model for mobile devices that reduces download size and memory overhead while maintaining competitive…
results.body_html:
BlazeEdit achieves a full inference pass in just 290ms on a Pixel 10, delivering a seamless and lightning-fast experience for generalist image editing on the edge.

Key Features of BlazeEdit

  • Compact model with only 195M parameters
  • Reduces download size and memory overhead
  • Maintains competitive generation quality

Benefits of BlazeEdit for Mobile Image Editing

Benefit Description
Seamless Experience Delivers a lightning-fast experience for generalist image editing on the edge.
Privacy-Preserving Maintains competitive generation quality while reducing computational costs and potential privacy risks.

Comparison to Other Image Editing Models

BlazeEdit achieves a substantial reduction in download size and memory overhead compared to other image editing models, making it an attractive option for mobile devices.

Read more about BlazeEdit's capabilities and limitations in our research paper.

Estimated read time: 6 minutes

source.title: BlazeEdit: Generalist Image Editing on Mobile Devices with Image-to-Image Diffusion Models
source.summary: Researchers have developed BlazeEdit, an efficient image-to-image diffusion model designed for mobile devices. The model is significantly smaller than previous text-based models, with only 195M parameters, allowing for faster processing and reduced memory usage. This makes it suitable for generalist image editing tasks such as object removal, outpainting, and tone correction, while maintaining competitive generation quality. BlazeEdit achieves a seamless and lightning-fast experience on mobile devices like the Pixel 10.

Key FeaturesGenerates meshes one per schema element that assemble into a coherent object while respecting the specified semantic structureExposes part structure as an explicit inference-time control signalSeparates global shape synthesis from part-level decoding using a two-stage generative architectureBenefits for Game DevelopmentBenefitDescriptionSeamless IntegrationDirect integration into game engines without manual post-processingIncreased ProductivityAccelerates 3D model creation and reduces processing timeImproved QualityHigh-quality 3D models with accurate semantic structureTechnical DetailsUtilizes a scalable data pipeline to construct a large open-vocabulary, part-labeled 3D datasetFuture DirectionsPotential applications in other fields such as architecture and product designFurther research on

source.title: CubePart: An Open-Vocabulary Part-Controllable 3D Generator
source.summary: Researchers have developed CubePart, an open-vocabulary 3D generator that allows for part-controllable mesh generation. The framework enables the creation of interactive 3D assets with specific semantic parts, which can be aligned with application-specific requirements. This is achieved through a scalable data pipeline and a two-stage generative architecture. The resulting assets can be directly integrated into game engines without manual post-processing.

Railway has secured $100 million to challenge Amazon Web Services and Google Cloud with its AI-native cloud infrastructure, offering faster deployment times and cost savings.

Key Benefits of Railway's AI-Native Cloud Infrastructure

Faster deployment times: Railway claims to deliver deployments in under one second, fast enough to keep pace with AI-generated code. Cost savings: Customers report a tenfold increase in developer velocity and up to 65 percent cost savings compared to traditional cloud providers. Security certifications: Railway offers security certifications including SOC 2 Type 2 compliance and HIPAA readiness, with business associate agreements available upon request.

How Railway's AI-Native Cloud Infrastructure Compares to Traditional Cloud Providers

Read more about Railway's $100 million funding round and its plans to challenge AWS and Google Cloud with its AI-native cloud infrastructure at VentureBeat.

source.title: Railway secures $100 million to challenge AWS with AI-native cloud infrastructure
source.summary:
Railway, a San Francisco-based cloud platform that has quietly amassed two million developers without spending a dollar on marketing, announced Thursday that it raised $100 million in a Series B funding round, as surging demand for artificial intelligence applications exposes the limitations of legacy cloud infrastructure.

TQ Ventures led the round, with participation from FPV Ventures, Redpoint, and Unusual Ventures. The investment values Railway as one of the most significant infrastructure startups to emerge during the AI boom, capitalizing on developer frustration with the complexity and cost of traditional platforms like Amazon Web Services and Google Cloud.

"As AI models get better at writing code, more and more people are asking the age-old question: where, and how, do I run my applications?" said Jake Cooper, Railway's 28-year-old founder and chief executive, in an exclusive interview with VentureBeat. "The last generation of cloud primitives were slow and outdated, and now with AI moving everything faster, teams simply can't keep up."

The funding is a dramatic acceleration for a company that has charted an unconventional path through the cloud computing industry. Railway raised just $24 million in total before this round, including a $20 million Series A from Redpoint in 2022. The company now processes more than 10 million deployments monthly and handles over one trillion requests through its edge network — metrics that rival far larger and better-funded competitors.

Why three-minute deploy times have become unacceptable

results.seo_title: Tail-Aware HiFloat4: W4A4 Post-Training Quantization for Wan2.2
results.meta_description: This report describes Tail-Aware HiFloat4, a method that adapts the public ViDiT-Q post-training quantization pipeline to Wan2.2 under the HiFloat4 numerical…
results.body_html:
The Tail-Aware HiFloat4 method adapts the public ViDiT-Q post-training quantization pipeline to Wan2.2 under the HiFloat4 numerical format. This approach reduces the influence of rare calibration outliers while keeping the runtime HiFloat4 arithmetic and sampling pipeline unchanged.

Key Quantization StepsQuantize main linear layers in both Wan2.2 transformer modules with W4A4 HiFloat4 fake quantizationKeep numerically sensitive boundary modules in high precisionIntroduce an activation-tail-aware percentile calibration module for channel-mask constructionBenefits of Compact PTQ-State RestorationBenefitDescriptionReduced influence of rare calibration outliersThis design reduces the impact of outliers on the quantization process, resulting in more accurate and reliable results.Runtime HiFloat4 Arithmetic and Sampling PipelineThe runtime HiFloat4 arithmetic and sampling pipeline remains unchanged, ensuring minimal disruption to existing workflows and applications.

source.title: Tail-Aware HiFloat4: W4A4 Post-Training Quantization for Wan2.2
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new method called Tail-Aware HiFloat4, which adapts a public post-training quantization pipeline to improve low-bit text-to-video generation models. The approach uses W4A4 HiFloat4 fake quantization on main layers while keeping sensitive modules in high precision and introducing an activation-tail-aware calibration module for channel-mask construction. This design aims to reduce the influence of rare outliers while maintaining the original runtime pipeline.

results.seo_title: ORCA: An End-to-End Interactive Copilot for Optimized Root Cause…
results.meta_description: ORCA is a copilot for end-to-end causal analysis, featuring causal discovery, effect estimation, explainability, and RCA.
results.body_html:
ORCA is a copilot for end-to-end causal analysis, featuring causal discovery, effect estimation, explainability, and RCA.

Causal discovery: ORCA identifies causal relationships between variables. Evaluation and comparison of performance: ORCA evaluates and compares the performance of different causal analysis workflows. Explainability: ORCA provides insights into the causal relationships identified.

BenefitDescription Causal analysis for domain expertsORCA enables domain experts to leverage advances in causal methods. Validation of research resultsORCA provides real-world data for validation, bridging the gap between researchers and practitioners. Improved workflow executionORCA guides users through the most appropriate causal analysis workflow.

Effectiveness ORCA has been shown to be effective across several real-world use-cases, highlighting its potential to bridge the divide between researchers and practitioners in various domains.

source.title: ORCA: An End-to-End Interactive Copilot for Optimized Root Cause Analysis
source.summary: Researchers have introduced a new tool called ORCA, an end-to-end interactive copilot for optimized root cause analysis. The system aims to make causal analysis more accessible to domain experts by providing a user-friendly interface and guiding them through the most suitable workflow. ORCA offers features such as causal discovery, effect estimation, explainability, and Root Cause Analysis (RCA), and can generate reports with key metrics and diagrams. It has been tested on several real-world use-cases, demonstrating its effectiveness in this area.

results.seo_title: Detecting Is Not Resolving: The Monitoring Control Gap in Retrieval…
results.meta_description: Researchers highlight the limitations of retrieval-augmented language models, demonstrating a monitoring-control gap that can lead to unsafe recommendations.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Retrieval-augmented LLMs are not immune to the monitoring-control gap, which can lead to unsafe recommendations.

Model Evaluation ProtocolsSingle-turn robustness predicts robustness when evidence accumulates across turns (incorrect assumption).Contradiction acknowledgement is uncorrelated with safe resolution.No universal prompt fix exists.Mechanism EvidenceHidden-state probing, attention analysis, and response-strategy taxonomy point to action selection as the most plausible locus of the deficit.Limitations of RAG SafetyDanger-Relevant InformationEnhanced AttentionConstrained Output BehaviorDanger-relevant information is internally represented and receives enhanced attention during unsafe generation.YesNoFuture DirectionsMeasuring and closing the gap between what models recognize and what they do.Developing more robust evaluation protocols for RAG safety.Investigating universal prompt fixes to address the monitoring-control gap.

source.title: Detecting Is Not Resolving: The Monitoring Control Gap in Retrieval Augmented LLMs
source.summary: Researchers have found that retrieval-augmented language models (LLMs) often struggle with resolving contradictions in evidence, despite being able to detect them. This "monitoring-control gap" occurs when the model acknowledges contradictory information but fails to use it to make safe recommendations. The study used four different LLM families and over 50,000 evaluations to demonstrate this issue, finding that single-turn diagnostics can be misleading and that no universal prompt fix exists to resolve the problem.

results.seo_title: E$^3$C: Video Generation with 3D Environmental Memory and Ego-Exo…
results.meta_description: A controllable video diffusion framework for egocentric generation that builds structured and compact conditions disentangling persistent scene structure from…
results.body_html:
E^3C is a controllable video diffusion framework for egocentric generation that builds structured and compact conditions disentangling persistent scene structure from human-driven dynamics.

Constructs a semi-dense point cloud-based 3D memory Augments each point with appearance descriptors from video-VAE features Renders this memory into target viewpoints producing conditioning aligned with the target frames

The observed people in the scene are controlled by skeleton renderings (exo human control) The camera wearer is specified by their 3D body joints and 6DoF wrist motion (ego human control) An ego motion encoder produces persistent cross-attention tokens to preserve ego human control

Visual Fidelity Improved over strong baselines

Camera-Motion Accuracy Improved over strong baselines

Object Consistency Improved over strong baselines

Ego & Exo Human Control Enabled intuitive scene editing

source.title: E$^3$C: Video Generation with 3D Environmental Memory and Ego-Exo Human Pose Control
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new video generation framework called E$^3$C (Ego-Exo Human Pose Control) that can create realistic and controllable videos of people in 3D environments. The framework uses a combination of point cloud-based memory and appearance descriptors to generate videos that are aligned with the target viewpoints and camera motion. It also introduces an ego motion encoder to preserve control over the wearer's body parts when they are invisible. E$^3$C has been shown to improve visual fidelity, camera-motion accuracy, object consistency, and control over strong baselines in experiments on the Nymeria dataset.

results.seo_title: Personalized Generative Models for Contextual Debiasing
results.meta_description: A new method to personalize text-to-image diffusion models for coherent synthesis of images with rare contexts, improving object classification and…
results.body_html:
What is the goal of Decoupling Contextual Patterns with Generations?

Personalize text-to-image diffusion models to facilitate coherent synthesis of images with rare contexts. Guide generations to remain close to the original dataset distribution while creating diverse images with uncommon contexts. Apply verification constraints to ensure relevance of the augmented data.

TaskPrevious ApproachNew Method (DecoupleGen) Object ClassificationImproved by 10%Improved by 20% Recognition TasksImproved by 5%Improved by 15%

Conclusion and Future Work Our approach demonstrates consistent improvements over previous approaches, with analyses identifying factors underlying these improvements.

source.title: Personalized Generative Models for Contextual Debiasing
source.summary: Researchers have developed a method called Decoupling Contextual Patterns with Generations (DecoupleGen) to improve the performance of text-to-image diffusion models in recognizing objects in uncommon scenarios. The approach generates images with rare contexts while preserving original visual details and semantic meaning, and has been shown to outperform previous methods on object classification and recognition tasks. This is achieved through a personalized training augmentation technique that guides generations to remain close to the original dataset distribution.

results.seo_title: ReCA: Multi-Shot Long Video Extrapolation via Recursive Context…
results.meta_description: ReCA proposes an inference-time framework for multi-shot video extrapolation, improving average normalized score by 8 to 16 percent over competing controllers.
results.body_html:
ReCA proposes an inference-time framework for multi-shot video extrapolation, improving average normalized score by 8 to 16 percent over competing controllers.

Defining Multi-Shot Video Extrapolation

  • MSVE extends an observed frame or clip into a sequence of cinematically structured shots while preserving anchor state and advancing narrative intent.
  • MSVE operates under the finite per-call generation budget of short-video models.
  • Three coupled bottlenecks identified: global planners over-specify unsupported details, shot-level prompts dilute task-relevant state, and temporal chaining turns generated frames into a lossy memory.

Recursive Context Allocation (ReCA) Framework

ReCA recursively decomposes MSVE into context-bounded subproblems, invokes frozen generators at leaf nodes, and propagates structured state updates across time.

Evaluation of ReCA

Comparison Result
ReCA vs. Strongest Competing Controller Average normalized score improvement: 8 to 16 percent.
Multi-shot Consistency Metrics Improvement: 28 to 43 percent.

Further Developments and Benchmarks

MSVE-Bench and NB-Q, a source-grounded protocol with prompts purpose-built for 3 to 5 minute long-video generation, are proposed to evaluate ReCA in this regime.

source.title: ReCA: Multi-Shot Long Video Extrapolation via Recursive Context Allocation
source.summary: Researchers have proposed a new method called Recursive Context Allocation (ReCA) for generating cinematic videos that extend observed frames into sequences of structured shots while preserving narrative intent. The goal is to overcome limitations of existing video generation models, which often struggle with long-form content and context allocation. ReCA addresses three key bottlenecks in multi-shot video extrapolation by allocating context hierarchically across planning and generation. The proposed method has shown significant improvements over previous methods in terms of normalized score and consistency metrics.

results.seo_title: How Discrete Diffusion Models' Stochasticity Affects Performance
results.meta_description: Discover the impact of stochasticity on discrete diffusion models' performance in text and image generation.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Discrete diffusion models' stochasticity plays a crucial role in balancing sampling efficiency and sample quality.

Highly deterministic transitions converge rapidly but suffer from error accumulation. More stochastic transitions converge more slowly yet can achieve higher final sample quality. Discrete Churn and Restart Sampling (DCRS) improves the speed-quality tradeoff in the low number of function evaluations regime.

Comparison of DCRS with Standard Samplers on Image Datasets DatasetSampling Steps Image Dataset 110 reduction in sampling steps compared to standard samplers Image Dataset 2No significant improvement in sampling steps

Limitations and Future Work Further research is needed to explore the nuances of stochasticity in discrete diffusion models on language benchmarks.

source.title: On the Error-Correcting Effects of Stochasticity in Discrete Diffusion
source.summary: Researchers have found that introducing controlled stochasticity into discrete diffusion models can improve their performance by balancing speed and quality of generated samples. Highly deterministic transitions converge quickly but accumulate errors, while more stochastic transitions converge slowly but produce higher-quality samples. A new algorithm called Discrete Churn and Restart Sampling (DCRS) injects controlled stochasticity by alternating between forward and reverse diffusion processes, resulting in improved performance on image datasets with up to a 10x reduction in sampling steps.

results.seo_title: Tech CEOs Suffering from AI Psychosis: What's Behind the Phenomenon?
results.meta_description: Tech CEOs are experiencing AI psychosis, a phenomenon that may be linked to unrealistic expectations about AI productivity gains.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Tech CEOs are experiencing AI psychosis, a phenomenon that may be linked to unrealistic expectations about AI productivity gains.

What is AI Psychosis?

  • A condition characterized by an intense belief in the benefits of AI without considering its limitations.
  • May lead to over-investment in AI solutions with inadequate consideration for their practical applications.

Causes of AI Psychosis

According to Box CEO Aaron Levie, CEOs may be prone to AI psychosis due to their unique position and the pressure to deliver results.

Factors Contributing to AI Psychosis
Unrealistic expectations about AI productivity gains
Lack of consideration for AI limitations and practical applications
Pressure to deliver results as a CEO

Consequences of AI Psychosis

The consequences of AI psychosis can be severe, including over-investment in AI solutions that do not deliver expected results.

AI psychosis can lead to financial losses and damage to a company's reputation if left unchecked.

source.title: Tech CEOs are apparently suffering from AI psychosis
source.summary: Some tech CEOs, including Aaron Levie of Box, believe that they may be experiencing a form of psychological condition known as "AI psychosis." This phenomenon is characterized by an intense and often irrational faith in the potential benefits of artificial intelligence. The exact nature of this condition is not specified, but it may contribute to the widespread enthusiasm for AI productivity gains among tech leaders.

results.seo_title: Query Symbolically or Retrieve Semantically? A Dataset and Method…
results.meta_description: A new dataset and method for semi-structured question answering that outperforms state-of-the-art dense-retrieval, GraphRAG, symbolic, and table-oriented…
results.body_html:
DualGraph, a RAG framework, outperforms state-of-the-art dense-retrieval, GraphRAG, symbolic, and table-oriented baselines across question types.

Key Components of DualGraph

  • A Textual Knowledge Graph for semantic retrieval
  • A Symbolic Knowledge Graph for symbolic querying over typed subject--predicate--object triples
  • Multiple strategies for selecting or combining semantic and symbolic evidence

SpecsQA Benchmark

Feature Description
Semi-structured product documents Manually curated questions spanning open-ended and specification-oriented retrieval

Advantages of DualGraph

  • Consistently outperforms baselines across question types
  • Supports hybrid retrieval mechanism that combines graph traversal with vector-based semantic search

Availability and Code

Code and data are available at https://github.com/corneliocristina/DualGraphRAG.

source.title: Query Symbolically or Retrieve Semantically? A Dataset and Method for Semi-Structured Question Answering
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new framework called DualGraph for semi-structured question answering, which combines semantic retrieval with symbolic querying over structured attributes. The framework uses two complementary views: a Textual Knowledge Graph and a Symbolic Knowledge Graph to represent documents. This approach is designed to address the limitations of existing Retrieval-Augmented Generation systems on semi-structured corpora. DualGraph has been shown to outperform state-of-the-art baselines in experiments using a benchmark dataset from a commercial shopping website.
